nativeRange.habitat: Stylocoeniella occurs on reef slopes, back-reef flats, and lagoonal and turbid habitats, where it encrusts hard substrate from shallow zones down to moderate depths.

decorPreferences: stable rockwork with open surface area for encrusting
avoid placement adjacent to aggressive LPS or sweeper-tentacle corals
allow growth room — fast encrusting spreader

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding of rotifers, amino acids, or small zooplankton is optional but has been reported to enhance growth and coloration. Captures dissolved organics and small particulates from the water column.

goodWith: other SPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles placed in direct contact
corals placed directly in its projected growth path

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; ~1200x887. In-situ small encrusting colony of Stylocoeniella armata, whole colony in frame, sharp, clearly showing the velvety spiny coenosteum texture. Best framing for a representative genus image.
Debra Baker; 2048x1536. Stylocoeniella guentheri, colorful yellow/tan/teal live colony edge showing thorn-like coenosteum spines and star-shaped polyps; sharp macro (second species in genus).
Debra Baker; 2048x1536. Stylocoeniella armata, vivid orange/teal live tissue with white star-shaped polyps; sharp diagnostic close-up (distinct color morph).
Debra Baker; 1602x2048. Stylocoeniella armata, vivid green/yellow live tissue with white star polyps; sharp, colorful; feature close-up.
Debra Baker; 2048x1536. Stylocoeniella guentheri, wider in-situ view showing encrusting growth habit over rock; shows how the colony spreads (second species, growth-habit context).

summary: The Stylocoeniella Coral is an encrusting small polyp stony (SPS) coral in the family Astrocoeniidae, less commonly kept than mainstream SPS but generally regarded as one of the more forgiving choices in the group. Its tightly packed polyps give colonies a velvety texture, and it is sometimes confused with Montipora or Porites.
careNotes:
The Stylocoeniella Coral belongs to the small and seldom-encountered family Astrocoeniidae — most aquarists work with Acropora, Montipora, and Porites long before coming across this genus. In the aquarium trade it is sometimes listed simply as 'Thorn Coral' or traded under designer-morph names such as 'Electric Daisy'. Wild imports are infrequent; most available specimens are aquacultured frags that circulate within the hobby.

Identification: When flow is low to moderate, the polyps extend and the colony can closely resemble certain Montipora or small-polyp Porites. In stronger flow the polyps retract and the flattened encrusting sheet can be mistaken for Montipora. Small frags have been documented being mislabeled as Montipora in the trade. Close inspection under a loupe reveals distinct thorn-like spines on the coenosteum (the hard skeletal tissue connecting the polyps) — the feature behind the 'Thorn Coral' common name — that are absent in Montipora and Porites. The family Astrocoeniidae contains only a handful of aquarium-relevant genera (Stylocoeniella, Palauastrea), making it one of the rarer coral families represented in reef tanks.

Lighting: Generally advised in the moderate-to-high range (~150–300 PAR). It is more tolerant of lower light than typical SPS and has been reported thriving in the 50–100 PAR range on tank walls or in partially shaded spots. Sudden exposure to high PAR tends to bleach frags; acclimate gradually. Brown coloration usually indicates insufficient light.

Flow: Moderate to moderately strong flow is generally recommended — enough to prevent detritus accumulating on the encrusting surface without blasting tissue directly. Gentle alternating flow from a wavemaker is preferred over direct laminar flow.

Growth: Fast-growing for an SPS. A small frag can encrust and spread across significant rockwork area within months under good conditions. Plan placement carefully — once established it is difficult to remove without damaging the host rock.

Disease resistance: Stylocoeniella is reportedly more resistant to Slow and Rapid Tissue Necrosis (STN/RTN) than many other SPS corals, which contributes to its relative ease of care despite being a stony coral.

Water chemistry: Stylocoeniella tends to tolerate a broader nutrient range than more demanding SPS such as Acropora. Nitrate 5–10 ppm and phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm are generally recommended, though it has been reported tolerating levels as high as 50 ppm nitrate and 2.5 ppm phosphate. Calcium ~400–450 ppm, kH 7–11 dKH, magnesium ~1300–1400 ppm.

Fragging: The encrusting base can be cut with a bandsaw or tile saw and frags readily attach to plugs or rubble. This ease of propagation is the primary reason Stylocoeniella has become more commercially available despite infrequent wild import.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard propagation method and is straightforward due to the encrusting growth form. A section of substrate with attached coral can be cut, glued to a frag plug, and expected to recover and grow. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced.

primary.notes: Primary source for PAR (150 moderate), kH (8-9 dKH), calcium (400-450), care level (beginner), temperature (82°F / ~28°C natural), family (Astrocoeniidae), feeding, native range (Indo-Pacific).

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Stylocoeniella Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: flow (low-medium), lighting (50-100 PAR lower bound), calcium (~425 ppm), alkalinity (7-11 dkH), magnesium (~1350 ppm), native range (Indonesia and Australia), confusion with Montipora. Recommended lower PAR than other sources — averaged/reconciled toward 150-300 range with note of low-end tolerance.

additional.name: Whitlyn Aquatics – Stylocoeniella Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature (74-80°F = 23-27°C), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (1.024-1.026), lighting (100-250 PAR), flow (moderate to strong), placement (rockwork with open space), confusion with Montipora and Goniopora noted.

additional.name: Frag Box – WWC Electric Daisy Stylocoeniella
additional.notes: Cross-checked: placement (middle to high), lighting (moderate to high), flow (moderate to high), native range (Indonesia and Great Barrier Reef), ease of fragging confirmed.

additional.name: WoRMS – Stylocoeniella guentheri
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmation: Order Scleractinia, Family Astrocoeniidae. Depth range 5-50 m. Distribution: Indo-Pacific including Australia, French Polynesia, Guam, Maldives, South China Sea.
